\newcommand{\clusteradjustment}{Standard errors are adjusted for stack-based duplication by dividing the variance-covariance matrix by the number of stacks $K$.}
\makeatother
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper discusses standard error inflation from stack-based observation duplication. Simply dividing the variance-covariance matrix by the number of stacks is mathematically incorrect for clustering; proper adjustment requires clustering at the unit or unit-by-stack level to account for the mechanical correlation introduced by duplication.

perturbations.perturbed:
\renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt} % no line
\newcommand{\saturatedFE}{Saturated fixed effects are specified such that unit-by-stack and time-by-stack effects are included, while omitting the base unit effects to ensure no collinearity drops occur in the presence of time-varying treatments.}
perturbations.why_wrong: In a stacked difference-in-differences design, including unit-by-stack and time-by-stack fixed effects while also attempting to estimate certain treatment effects can lead to unintended collinearity or the dropping of the very variation the model intends to identify, especially if the 'clean comparison' group is not correctly partitioned.

perturbations.perturbed: % Violation of clean comparison: including previously treated units in the control group of subsequent stacks to maximize sample size.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper emphasizes the 'clean comparison' requirement. Including previously treated units in the control group of a later stack violates the parallel trends assumption and introduces bias, as the control group is no longer 'never-treated' or 'not-yet-treated' within the local window of the stack.

perturbations.original:
\newtheoremstyle{rhtheorem}% <name>
{5.5pt plus 2.0pt minus 4.0pt}
{10.0pt plus 2.0pt minus 4.0pt}
%{\topsep}% measure of space to leave above the theorem. E.g.: 3pt

{\thmname{#1}\thmnumber{ #2}\textnormal{\thmnote{ (#3)}}}
perturbations.why_wrong: Misaligning event-time windows across cohorts with different treatment durations leads to an unbalanced panel within stacks, which can bias the aggregated lead and lag coefficients if the composition of cohorts changes at different event-time distances.

The paper notes an inconsistency between theoretical aggregation weights and empirical regression weights. Standard OLS on a stacked dataset does not automatically recover the desired theoretical weights (like population-weighted ATT) unless specific weights are applied to the regression.

This is factually incorrect in the context of the paper's focus on econometrics. Duplicating observations (stacking) artificially inflates the sample size, leading to underestimated standard errors if one does not account for the fact that the same unit-period observation appears multiple times.

The paper discusses how incorrect specification of saturated fixed effects leads to collinearity. Interacting every unit and time dummy with stack indicators in a model with a constant or overlapping treatment periods will lead to a rank-deficient matrix where many indicators are dropped.

This violates the 'clean comparison' requirement. Units that were treated earlier in the panel carry over treatment effects that can contaminate the counterfactual for the current cohort, even if they are not 'currently' receiving a new treatment change in that window.

The paper highlights the inconsistency between empirical OLS weights (which are variance-weighted) and theoretical weights (which are usually population or size-weighted). OLS weights do not naturally match the simple average treatment effect in the presence of heterogeneous effects.

The stacks are not independent because they are constructed by duplicating the same underlying observations from the original panel. This duplication creates mechanical correlation across stacks, necessitating clustered standard errors at the unit level.
